reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No mandatory style violations: CodeBlock is correctly used with `intellisense` and `label: 'TypeScript'`, bash blocks each contain a single command, no YAML Stacktape config anywhere.

The 'What runs where' table is the strongest section on the page — it immediately answers the first question a new user has and is well-grounded in source (container, function, hosting-bucket, nextjs-web, ssr-web categories all confirmed in source).
Terminal commands table (`rs`, `rs myApi`, `c`, `q`) is accurate and source-grounded (handleCommand function in index.ts), highly practical for first-time users.
FAQ quality is strong: 8 Q&As cover Stacktape-specific behavior, AWS-service questions (Docker, billing), and comparison questions. The depth per answer is appropriate.
The skip-ahead `<Info>` callout and the closing next-step link are both present as required by section instructions.
Agent mode default port (7331) is directly confirmed in source and the section is clearly marked as optional/advanced.
Database Docker image mapping in the emulation table is well-grounded in the `mapResourceType` function.
problems: The 'Start dev mode' section claims first-run takes '1-2 minutes' — this timing estimate is not present in any provided source file. It is an invented number.

The Useful Flags table lists short aliases (--res, -sr, -w, -rr, -nt, -dm) that are not confirmed in the provided source files. If any of these are wrong, a first-time user will be confused when the flag is rejected.
The FAQ answer 'Stage names must be at most 10 characters' comes from MEMORY.md, not from the provided source code. It may be correct but is not grounded in the inline sources.

The Environment Variables section shows env var names like STP_MY_DATABASE_HOST and STP_MY_DATABASE_PORT as if they are guaranteed injected vars. The source grounding verifier may flag these as unsupported — the source only shows the password/host/port used internally by the agent API, not what is injected into workload processes.

The page significantly exceeds the 400-800 word prose target for getting-started pages. Agent mode, Debugging, and Legacy mode sections introduce complexity that is not part of a first-timer's core experience and dilutes the dev-mode 'wow moment' that step 2 is supposed to deliver.

Section instructions mention screenshots as a content type alongside code blocks — the page has none, which is a missed opportunity for showing the TUI/interactive picker that is central to the dev-mode experience.
optionalImprovements: Remove or replace the '1-2 minutes' timing estimate with a qualitative description ('a short initial deployment') since no timing source exists.
Verify each flag alias (-res, -sr, -w, -rr, -nt, -dm) against the actual CLI arg definitions before publishing; remove any that cannot be confirmed.
Replace 'Stage names must be at most 10 characters' in the FAQ with a softer 'keep stage names short' or verify it against the CLI validation source.

Scope the Environment Variables section to only claim the CONNECTION_STRING var (confirmed as the canonical connectTo injection) and use '$ResourceParam()' references for HOST/PORT rather than asserting they are auto-injected.

Move 'Agent mode for AI coding assistants', 'Debugging', and 'Legacy mode' sections to an expandable callout or a linked advanced page to keep the getting-started page focused on the first-time 'wow moment' flow.

Add at least one screenshot or animated GIF of the interactive resource picker or the running TUI dashboard — this is what makes dev mode visually compelling and the section instructions explicitly mention screenshots.

In 'The edit-save-see loop', consider foregrounding the actual feedback loop more viscerally: what the terminal looks like after an edit, what output the user sees. Right now the numbered steps describe what happens but don't show it.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript — no YAML config blocks anywhere on the page.
Every bash block contains exactly one command with no inline # explanatory comments, satisfying the copy-button discipline.

The 'What runs where' table is precise and source-grounded: hosting buckets correctly gated on the `dev` config property (matching `filter((b) => b.dev)` in source), Lambda functions correctly described as AWS-deployed with tunneling.
Terminal commands table (`rs`, `rs myApi`, `c/clear`, `q/quit`) matches the `handleCommand` implementation in the source exactly.
Local database emulation table (Docker images per engine type) and the `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` persistence path are both confirmed by source.
Agent mode default port (7331) is source-confirmed from `const defaultAgentPort = 7331`.
Default credential fallback password `localdevpassword` in the environment variables example is confirmed by `agent-server.ts`.
Skip-ahead `<Info>` block and 'Next →' footer link both present as required by getting-started section instructions.
CodeBlock class names (`LambdaFunction`, `StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`, `RelationalDatabase`, `RdsEnginePostgresql16`) all satisfy the documented class-name conventions.
The interactive resource picker and `--resources all` shortcut are accurately described, matching `tuiManager.promptMultiSelect` and the `resourcesSet.has('all')` branch in source.
problems: INVENTED URL: The Agent mode section links to `/using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev`, a route that does not appear in any provided source file. The curated docs reference `/getting-started/using-with-ai` instead. A broken cross-link on a getting-started page undermines trust immediately.

UNVERIFIED CLI ALIASES: The Useful Flags table lists short aliases `-res`, `-sr`, `-w`, `-rr`, `-nt`, `-dm` for the long-form flags. None of these aliases appear in the provided source files (`src/commands/dev/index.ts` or `agent-server.ts`). The CLI argument definitions are not in the supplied source. If any alias is wrong, a developer copy-pasting it will get a CLI error at the most critical onboarding moment.

INVENTED TIMING: 'On first run…This takes 1-2 minutes.' No timing data exists in the provided source files. This is a fabricated number; dev stack deployment time varies with region, account state, and stack size. This is an invented claim that should be removed.

IMPRECISE CLAIM: 'Subsequent runs skip this step and start instantly.' The source shows the existing stack is checked and metadata is still loaded from AWS (`initializeStackServicesForDevPhase2`), so 'instantly' is inaccurate. The correct claim is that the dev stack deployment is skipped.

ENV VAR OVERCLAIMING: Showing `STP_MY_DATABASE_HOST=localhost` and `STP_MY_DATABASE_PORT=5432` as separate injected env vars alongside the connection string is not confirmed by the connectTo JSDoc or any provided source. Per the style guide, document only confirmed injected vars; for others say they are available via `$ResourceParam()`.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the broken `/using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev` link with the source-confirmed path `/getting-started/using-with-ai`, or remove the cross-link and keep only the prose description of the feature. Broken links on a getting-started page are publication blockers.

Remove all unverified short aliases from the Useful Flags table (`-res`, `-sr`, `-w`, `-rr`, `-nt`, `-dm`). The provided source files do not confirm these aliases. Show only the long-form flags that are verifiable, and note 'aliases available via `stacktape help dev`' if you want to hint that aliases exist without inventing them.

Remove the invented '1-2 minutes' timing estimate from the 'Start dev mode' section. Replace with a factual description: 'On first run Stacktape deploys a minimal dev stack to AWS; this is skipped on subsequent runs when the stack already exists.' Do not substitute another invented estimate.

Scope the Environment Variables example to the confirmed `STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING` only, or explicitly mark `STP_MY_DATABASE_HOST` and `STP_MY_DATABASE_PORT` as 'available via `$ResourceParam()`' rather than claiming they are auto-injected, since the connectTo JSDoc does not enumerate them for relational databases.
optionalImprovements: The getting-started section instructions target 400–800 words plus code blocks. This page is roughly 1,500+ words of prose. Consider trimming the Useful Flags table to the 4–5 most commonly needed flags and moving the full flag reference to the CLI docs (`/cli/dev`). The getting-started page should leave the reader wanting to explore, not exhausted.

No screenshots or TUI terminal output illustrations are present. The section instructions call for screenshots; even a static terminal recording of the interactive resource picker and the running dev mode TUI would significantly improve the 'wow moment' this page is supposed to deliver.

The `--watch` flag is used in examples and described but never explained in terms of what 'file change' means — watched paths, debounce behavior, exclusions. A one-sentence note ('Stacktape watches the source files referenced in your packaging config') would prevent confusion when changes don't trigger a rebuild.

'Subsequent runs skip this step and start instantly' (after the mandatory fix removing 'instantly') could be strengthened by explaining that the dev stack is identified by a CloudFormation metadata tag (`isDevStack`), so Stacktape never accidentally treats a production stack as a dev stack.

The `--remoteResources` flag (connecting to deployed AWS resources instead of local emulation) is useful but mentioned only in the flags table with one sentence. A brief one-paragraph explanation of when to use it (e.g., testing against real Aurora instead of local Postgres, or when Docker is unavailable) would help developers decide whether to reach for it.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The 'What runs where' table is the standout section — concrete, source-grounded (matches `mapResourceType` in the source), and covers all resource categories including SSR variants (Astro, Nuxt, SvelteKit, SolidStart, TanStack, Remix). This is a highly citable table.
First paragraph (50 words) is self-contained and answers what dev mode is and why it matters, suitable for AI Overview extraction.

Agent mode HTTP server default port (7331) and all terminal commands (`rs`, `rs myApi`, `c`/`clear`, `q`/`quit`) are directly verified from the `commandDev` and `handleCommand` source — no invented details.

No forbidden patterns: config example uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` correctly with `label: 'TypeScript'`, `defineConfig`, and class-based resources. No YAML config, no `StacktapeConfig` type pattern.
Skip-ahead `<Info>` link and 'Next step' footer satisfy the getting-started page linear-flow requirements explicitly called out in the section instructions.
Local database emulation table matches the Docker image types in the source's `mapResourceType` function and the curated docs — mariadb distinction included.

FAQ is 8 questions and covers multiple categories: Stacktape-specific (Docker requirement, tunneling mechanism, config changes, monorepo, data persistence), comparison (dev mode vs deploy), and cost (AWS Lambda billing).
The 'Useful flags' table and 'Terminal commands' table together give the reader a complete reference without burying it in prose — scannability is high.
problems: Property-value anchor missing: the code example uses `instanceSize: 'db.t4g.micro'` on `RdsEnginePostgresql16` but the surrounding prose never explains what `instanceSize` controls or what alternative values exist. Per the mandatory reviewer criterion, this unexplained tunable value is a deduction.

Flag aliases (`-res`, `-sr`, `-w`, `-rr`, `-nt`, `-dm`) in the 'Useful flags' table are not confirmed by any of the provided source files. The `commandDev` source references `globalStateManager.args.resources`, `globalStateManager.args.noTunnel`, etc. but the CLI arg definitions (short aliases) are not in the provided sources. Documenting unverified aliases is a source-grounding risk.

The `$Stage()` Stacktape directive in the code example (`value: '$Stage()'`) is shown without any explanation. For a getting-started audience encountering Stacktape syntax for the first time, this reads as magic.

FAQ lacks use-case questions about the underlying AWS service mechanics — specifically, no question covers what the dev stack actually creates in AWS (IAM roles, API gateways) or how the Lambda tunnel works at the network level. The FAQ criterion requires mixing in AWS-service-level questions.

The 'Debugging' section is thin: two bullet links with no context for when each is appropriate. A reader doesn't know whether to reach for `debug:logs` vs `debug:metrics` without a one-line situational hint.
mandatoryFixes: Add 1-2 prose sentences near the code example explaining `instanceSize: 'db.t4g.micro'` — that it controls the RDS compute tier and that alternatives (e.g., `db.t4g.small`, `db.t4g.medium`) exist for workloads that need more headroom. Without this, readers cannot tell if `db.t4g.micro` is the minimum, the recommended default, or just a placeholder.

Either verify the short flag aliases (`-res`, `-sr`, `-w`, `-rr`, `-nt`, `-dm`) against the actual CLI argument definition source file (not provided here) or remove the alias column from the 'Useful flags' table entirely. If the aliases are wrong, developers will get CLI errors that contradict the docs — a particularly bad experience for a getting-started page.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence inline note beside `value: '$Stage()'` in the code example (or in the surrounding prose) identifying it as a Stacktape built-in expression that resolves to the current stage name — first-time readers won't recognise this syntax.

Add 1-2 FAQ questions about the AWS layer: e.g., 'What does the dev stack actually deploy to AWS?' (answer: IAM roles, Secrets Manager entries, and API Gateway routes — a lightweight scaffold) and 'How does the Lambda tunnel reach my local database?' (answer: Stacktape establishes a secure tunnel so the AWS Lambda function can call `localhost` on your machine).

Expand the 'Debugging' section by one sentence per command indicating the concrete scenario: `debug:logs` when you need to trace a Lambda invocation failure; `debug:metrics` when you want to inspect invocation count or duration trends.

The 'What you need' section could mention that stage names must be at most 10 characters — this is a common stumble point that prevents the first `stacktape dev` from running, and it appears in the deploy command docs anyway.

The 'The edit-save-see loop' H2 reads slightly like a sentence fragment. 'Edit-save loop' or 'Iteration loop' would be more compact and match the noun-form H2 style guide guidance, though this is minor.

issues.statement: If the dev stack needs updating (new IAM permissions, new API gateway routes), Stacktape redeploys it automatically on the next start.
issues.reason: The source code shows deployDevStack() is only called when the stack does not exist (`if (!stackManager.existingStackDetails)`). When an existing dev stack is found and recognized as a dev stack (`isDevStack` is true), the code simply proceeds without redeploying. There is no automatic update mechanism. A user who adds new IAM permissions or API gateway routes would NOT get an updated dev stack unless they delete the existing one first.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the sentence with: 'If you change your config in a way that affects the dev stack (new resources, new permissions), delete the existing dev stack with `stacktape delete --stage dev --region eu-west-1` and restart dev mode to create a fresh one.'

evidence.quote:
// Deploy dev stack if it doesn't exist
    if (!stackManager.existingStackDetails) {

issues.statement: Container workloads (web-service, worker-service, multi-container-workload) — Docker on your machine
issues.reason: The 'What runs where' table lists three container workload parent types but omits private-service. The source code's `allContainerWorkloads` getter explicitly includes `this.privateServices` alongside web-service, worker-service, and multi-container-workload. A reader with a private-service resource would incorrectly conclude dev mode doesn't support it.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the table row to: 'Container workloads (web-service, private-service, worker-service, multi-container-workload)'

issues.statement: On first run, Stacktape deploys a lightweight 'dev stack' to AWS — just IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways. This takes 1-2 minutes.
issues.reason: The '1-2 minutes' deployment time is not supported by the source. The only time reference in the source is a 10-minute timeout for agent mode daemon startup (`timeoutMs: 600000 // 10 minutes for stack deployment + startup`). The actual deployment time depends on the config and could be significantly longer. Stating a specific time sets incorrect expectations.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific time claim. Change to: 'On first run, Stacktape deploys a lightweight dev stack to AWS — IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways needed for local development. Subsequent runs detect the existing dev stack and start immediately.'

evidence.quote: timeoutMs: 600000 // 10 minutes for stack deployment + startup

issues.statement: Stage names must be at most 10 characters.
issues.reason: This constraint appears in the FAQ section but is not present in any of the provided source files for this page. The stage name length limit may exist elsewhere in the codebase but cannot be verified from the page's source. Stating it as fact in a getting-started page without source backing could mislead users.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the specific character limit claim, or soften to: 'Each developer should use a unique, short stage name (e.g., `--stage dev-alice`, `--stage dev-bob`).' If the constraint is important, link to a page where it's documented.

evidence.quote: hint: `Use a different stage name for dev mode (e.g., --stage dev-${globalStateManager.userData?.name?.split(' ')[0]?.toLowerCase() || 'local'}), or use '--devMode legacy' to run against an existing stack.`

issues.statement: Subsequent runs skip this step and start instantly.
issues.reason: While the dev stack deployment is skipped on subsequent runs (confirmed by source), the word 'instantly' overstates the speed. The source shows Phase 1 (credentials and config initialization) and Phase 2 (loading metadata from AWS) still run on every invocation, including spinner display for AWS metadata loading. These steps involve network calls to AWS.
issues.suggestedFix: Change 'start instantly' to 'start much faster' or 'skip this step and reuse the existing dev stack'.

issues.statement: An AWS account connected (dev mode deploys a minimal stack for IAM roles and API gateways)
issues.reason: The prerequisites section says 'IAM roles and API gateways' but the body text later says 'IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways'. The dev-stack-deployer.ts confirms secrets handling is part of the flow (ensureMissingSecretsCreated is called). The inconsistency within the same page could confuse readers.
issues.suggestedFix: Align the prerequisites description with the body text: 'An AWS account connected (dev mode deploys a minimal stack for IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways)'.

evidence.quote:
await ensureMissingSecretsCreated();
  await ensureMissingSsmParamsCreated();

positiveFindings: All CLI flag names and aliases verified against src/config/cli/options.ts — --resources/-res, --skipResources/-sr, --watch/-w, --remoteResources/-rr, --noTunnel/-nt, --devMode/-dm all match exactly.
The interactive resource picker behavior is accurately described — source confirms promptMultiSelect with all resources selected by default.
The terminal commands (rs, rs <name>, c/clear, q/quit) are all verified against the handleCommand function in the source.
Agent mode documentation is accurate — default port 7331 confirmed, --agent flag confirmed, HTTP API endpoints match the source.
Local database emulation types (postgres, mysql/mariadb, redis, dynamodb, opensearch) accurately match the mapResourceType function.
Legacy mode behavior correctly described — requires existing stack, throws clear error if stack doesn't exist.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index.

The CodeBlock example uses valid Stacktape class names (LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging, RelationalDatabase, RdsEnginePostgresql16) with proper defineConfig pattern and focus markers.
The freshDb flag and remoteResources behavior accurately described based on source and curated docs.
The hosting bucket dev config filter is correctly noted — source confirms hostingBuckets.filter((b) => b.dev).
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and mostly accurate for a getting-started page. The most significant issue is an incorrect FAQ claim that Stacktape automatically redeploys the dev stack when config changes — the source clearly shows the dev stack is only deployed when it doesn't exist, meaning users must manually delete and recreate it after config changes. The 'What runs where' table omits private-service from the container workload list despite the source explicitly including it. The '1-2 minutes' deployment time and '10 characters' stage name limit are unsupported numeric claims. CLI flags, terminal commands, database emulation types, agent mode details, and cross-links are all verified correct.

The draft is broadly aligned with the supplied dev-mode sources, but it overstates several timing, locality, pricing, and environment-variable details that the source does not support. The most important fixes are to narrow claims about first-run duration, exact dev-stack contents, local database paths/ports/credentials, and AWS-deployed Lambda behavior.

issues.statement: “On first run, Stacktape deploys a lightweight "dev stack" to AWS — just IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways. This takes 1-2 minutes. Subsequent runs skip this step and start instantly.”
issues.reason: The source supports deploying a dev stack when no existing dev stack exists, but it does not support the exact contents as “just” those resources, the 1-2 minute duration, or “start instantly” on later runs. The command still loads AWS metadata, checks secrets/SSM params, starts local resources, and runs workloads.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that normal dev mode deploys a minimal dev stack when needed, then starts selected workloads/local resources. Remove the 1-2 minute and “instantly” claims unless a timing source is provided.

evidence.quote:
// Deploy dev stack if it doesn't exist
    if (!stackManager.existingStackDetails) {

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly says Lambda functions are “deployed to AWS” in dev mode and that dev mode “deploys the Lambda function to AWS with a tunnel back to your local database.”
issues.reason: The source confirms functions are dev-compatible workloads and that Lambda environment variables/logs/tunnels are handled, but the provided code does not show that dev mode deploys each Lambda function as a normal AWS Lambda workload. The curated doc says Lambda functions are deployed to AWS, but source code is authoritative and the actual mechanism is not visible in the supplied implementation excerpt.
issues.suggestedFix: Use cautious wording grounded in the source: “Dev mode handles Lambda functions as function workloads, can stream their logs, and can set up tunnels so Lambda workloads can reach selected local resources.” Avoid saying each function is deployed to AWS unless the source list includes the runner/deployer code that proves it.

issues.statement: “Dev mode automatically starts Docker containers for your databases” and “Database data persists between sessions in `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/`.”
issues.reason: The source supports registering local resources and the agent startup message states that `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` persists between runs. The source does not show that every supported local resource is implemented as a Docker container, and it does not prove the exact directory is used for non-agent dev mode output beyond the startup message.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the persistence path, but phrase Docker more narrowly: “Dev mode starts local emulators for supported database-like resources; the curated docs describe these as Docker-backed local emulators.” If keeping the exact path, tie it to the startup output: “Dev mode reports local data under `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/`.”

evidence.quote: lines.push(`Data:  .stacktape/dev-data/${stage}/ (persists between runs, use --freshDb to reset)`);

issues.statement: The environment variable example gives exact values: `STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING=postgresql://postgres:localdevpassword@localhost:5432/postgres`, `STP_MY_DATABASE_HOST=localhost`, and `STP_MY_DATABASE_PORT=5432`.
issues.reason: The source supports default PostgreSQL credentials of `postgres` / `localdevpassword` / `postgres` inside the agent server connection helper, and supports `STP_*` env inspection. It does not support a fixed port of 5432 for all dev-mode runs; local resources use an `actualPort`, which can vary.
issues.suggestedFix: Use placeholders for host and port, or say the exact port is assigned by dev mode and visible in the dev-mode UI/agent status. Keep the resource-name-derived variable names only where the example resource name proves them.

evidence.quote: port: instance.actualPort,

user: instance.credentials?.username || 'postgres',
      password: instance.credentials?.password || 'localdevpassword',
      database: instance.dbName || 'postgres'

issues.statement: “With this config, `stacktape dev ... --resources all` ... injects environment variables like `STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING` pointing to the local Postgres.”
issues.reason: The example uses `connectTo: [myDatabase]`, so the generated resource-name-dependent variable name is plausible. However, the provided source for dev mode does not show which `connectTo` variables are injected, and the agent endpoint only exposes whatever environment variables exist. The claim should not imply the dev command itself proves this env-var mapping without the connectTo source file.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the `ResourceAccessProps.connectTo` source to the source set or narrow the claim: “The workload environment can be inspected with the agent `/env/{workload}` endpoint; for connected databases, use the documented `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern from the connecting-resources page.”

issues.statement: “Stage names must be at most 10 characters.”
issues.reason: No supplied source file defines a 10-character limit for stage names. The dev command uses the stage name for stack targeting and path display, but no validation constraint appears in the provided sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the 10-character sentence, or replace it with a sourced stage-naming rule if the relevant config/validation file is added.

stageName: globalStateManager.targetStack.stage,

issues.statement: “This starts an HTTP server (default port 7331) with endpoints for status checks, rebuilds, log access, and direct database queries.”
issues.reason: The default port and status/rebuild/log/database endpoints are supported. The phrase omits that the source also exposes environment-variable inspection, stop, health, OpenSearch/DynamoDB/Redis-specific endpoints, and direct AWS SDK access; for an agent-mode section, this is materially incomplete because the source exposes a broader control surface.
issues.suggestedFix: Broaden the sentence: “The agent server starts on port 7331 by default, or the next available port, and exposes status, logs, environment variables, rebuild, stop, local-resource health, database/query helpers, and AWS SDK endpoints.”

evidence.quote: const defaultAgentPort = 7331;

issues.statement: The “Useful flags” table lists short aliases such as `-res`, `-sr`, `-rr`, `-nt`, and `-dm`.
issues.reason: The provided source files use long argument names from `globalStateManager.args`, but the CLI command-definition source that would prove aliases is not included. The aliases may exist, but they are not grounded in the supplied sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove aliases from this Getting Started page unless `src/config/cli/commands.ts` or equivalent CLI definition source is added. The long flags are enough for a hands-on onboarding page.

positiveFindings: The draft uses the required TypeScript `defineConfig` / class-based Stacktape config inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author YAML.

The resource picker, `--resources all`, comma-separated resource selection, `--skipResources`, and terminal commands `rs`, `rs <name>`, `c`/`clear`, and `q`/`quit` match the supplied dev command source.
The agent-mode default-port behavior is broadly grounded: the source uses port 7331 by default and searches the next available port when needed.
The draft preserves the required Getting Started flow by linking to the previous configuration step near the top and ending with the next deployment step.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main dev-mode workflow well, but it makes several broad claims that are not grounded in the provided command source and would likely fail source-grounding. The most important fixes are to narrow claims about where resources run, Lambda behavior, local database endpoints, and AWS prerequisites to what the provided source actually shows.

issues.statement: "An AWS account connected (dev mode deploys a minimal stack for IAM roles and API gateways)"
issues.reason: The source shows dev mode initializes AWS metadata and may deploy a dev stack, but the provided source does not establish an onboarding prerequisite that an AWS account must already be connected, nor does it enumerate the dev stack as specifically IAM roles and API gateways.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: "Dev mode initializes AWS credentials and metadata, and in normal mode deploys a dev stack when one does not already exist." Avoid listing exact dev-stack contents unless backed by deploy-dev-stack source.

evidence.quote: await initializeStackServicesForDevPhase1();
if (!stackManager.existingStackDetails) { ... await deployDevStack(); }

issues.statement: "On first run, Stacktape deploys a lightweight 'dev stack' to AWS — just IAM roles, secrets, and API gateways. This takes 1-2 minutes. Subsequent runs skip this step and start instantly."
issues.reason: The code supports deploying a dev stack when no stack exists and skipping deployment when an existing dev stack is found. It does not support the exact resource list, the 1-2 minute duration, or the claim that later starts are instant.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: "On first run in normal mode, Stacktape deploys a dev stack if one does not exist. Later runs reuse an existing dev stack when it is still marked as a dev stack."

evidence.quote: // Normal mode: check for dev stack or deploy one
if (stackManager.existingStackDetails) { const isDevStack = deployedStackOverviewManager.getStackMetadata(stackMetadataNames.isDevStack());

issues.statement: "Lambda functions | Deployed to AWS | Repackaged and code-updated on file change or manual trigger"
issues.reason: The source comment says dev mode starts local workloads and "Deploy and stream logs for functions", but the provided code does not show Lambda functions being code-updated on file change. The rebuild handler can rebuild workloads, but the implementation details are in imported functions not provided here.
issues.suggestedFix: Use source-grounded wording: "Lambda functions are handled as function workloads in dev mode; dev mode can rebuild all workloads or a named workload through the TUI command handler."

evidence.quote: const functions = configManager.functions.map((f) => ({ name: f.name, type: 'function' as const, category: 'function' as const }));
if (command === 'rs') { ... await rebuildAllWorkloads(); }

issues.statement: "Local workloads receive the same `STP_*` environment variables they would get in a deployed stack, but pointing to local endpoints."
issues.reason: The agent server exposes environment variables for workloads and Lambda functions, and dev mode has an "Injecting environment" setup step. The provided source does not prove parity with deployed stacks or the exact `STP_*` variables for local workloads.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement: "Dev mode tracks environment variables for selected workloads. In agent mode, `GET /env/{workload}` returns the variables Stacktape has for that workload."

evidence.quote: addRoute('GET', '/env/:workload', async ({ pathParams }) => {
devTuiManager.addSetupStep('env-inject', 'Injecting environment');

issues.statement: "With this config, `stacktape dev ...` spins up a local PostgreSQL container accessible at `localhost:5432`."
issues.reason: The source maps relational databases to local resource type `postgres`, but actual host and port come from the local resource instance. The agent source exposes `instance.host` and `instance.actualPort`, which means the port is not guaranteed to be 5432 by the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: "Dev mode registers the database as a local PostgreSQL resource. Use the dev-mode output or agent `/health/{name}` endpoint to see the actual host and port."

evidence.quote: return 'postgres'; // Default for postgres, aurora-postgres, etc.
port: instance.actualPort, host: instance.host

issues.statement: "This starts an HTTP server (default port 7331) with endpoints for status checks, rebuilds, log access, and direct database queries."
issues.reason: The default port is grounded, as are status, rebuild, logs, and database endpoints. The draft omits important source-grounded agent capabilities that are likely useful in this section: environment-variable inspection, health checks, stop, and AWS SDK access.
issues.suggestedFix: Expand the agent-mode section to mention `GET /status`, `GET /logs`, `GET /env/{workload}`, `GET /health/{resource}`, `POST /rebuild/{workload}`, `POST /stop`, database endpoints, and `POST /aws/sdk`. Keep detailed endpoint docs deferred to the dedicated page.

evidence.quote: docs['core'] = { 'GET  /status': '?verbose=true (optional)', 'GET  /logs': 'Returns JSONL log file path (use tail/grep/jq)', 'GET  /env/:workload': '', 'GET  /health/:resource': 'Check health of local resource', 'POST /rebuild/:workload': '{} or use /rebuild/all', 'POST /stop': '{}' };
docs['aws'] = { 'POST /sdk': '{service: "lambda", command: "ListFunctions", input?: {...}, region?}', 'GET  /sdk/services': 'List supported services and example commands' };

issues.statement: "Each stage gets its own isolated dev stack and local data directory. Stage names must be at most 10 characters."
issues.reason: The source shows the local data directory includes the stage, and the command header includes stage, but the provided source does not include a stage-name length constraint or a general isolation guarantee for every stage.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the 10-character limit unless another source file proves it. Write: "Use separate stage names so dev mode targets separate Stacktape stacks and separate `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` directories."

evidence.quote: Data:  .stacktape/dev-data/${stage}/ (persists between runs, use --freshDb to reset)
stageName: globalStateManager.targetStack.stage

issues.statement: "Dev mode splits your stack between local execution and AWS, depending on resource type" followed by table entries for all container workloads, Lambda functions, SSR frontends, hosting buckets, and databases.
issues.reason: The source shows these resource categories are selectable, but selected resources are controlled by `--resources`, `--skipResources`, or the interactive picker. The table currently reads like all matching resources always run in dev mode.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence before the table: "Only selected resources run; by default the picker selects all dev-compatible resources, while `--resources` and `--skipResources` narrow the selection."

if (resourcesSet.has('all')) { let selected = new Set(allNames);

positiveFindings: The page avoids forbidden hand-authored YAML and uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with `label: 'TypeScript'` for the Stacktape config example.
The draft preserves the getting-started flow with a skip-ahead link near the top and a next-step link to `/getting-started/deploy-your-first-stage`.
The command examples follow the one-command-per-shell-block rule and avoid shell comments inside copyable command blocks.
The resource-selection behavior, interactive picker, `rs` rebuild command, `c`/`clear`, and `q`/`quit` terminal commands are consistent with the provided `commandDev` source.

verifierResults.summary: Deterministic check of <CodeBlock intellisense> Stacktape config examples and generated MDX component usage.

issues.statement: <CodeBlock intellisense> #1 (TypeScript): cannot generate YAML tab from TypeScript source.
issues.reason: The TypeScript example parsed and type-checked, but the docs YAML converter could not derive a YAML tab. This may be a docs-converter coverage gap for valid class-based config.
issues.suggestedFix: If the example is valid Stacktape TypeScript, extend the YAML converter for this class/property shape. Otherwise simplify the example to a converter-supported class-based defineConfig shape.

issues.statement: <CodeBlock intellisense> #1 (TypeScript): `new RdsEnginePostgresql16(...)` references an unknown class.
issues.reason: `RdsEnginePostgresql16` is not exported by the `stacktape` package.
issues.suggestedFix: Use one of the documented Stacktape resource classes (e.g. LambdaFunction, WebService, RelationalDatabase). If `RdsEnginePostgresql16` is meant to be a custom class, define it in the example or remove the example.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Single-command-per-block pattern is followed throughout — no copy-button trap anywhere on the page.
The 'What runs where' table is excellent: it sets accurate expectations about Lambda vs. container execution models before the reader has hit any surprises.
CodeBlock with intellisense is used correctly for the Stacktape config example — no forbidden YAML block or plain TypeScript fence.
All H2 headings are noun-form, 1-5 words, and genuinely descriptive.
Terminal commands table (rs, rs <name>, c/clear, q/quit) is directly grounded in source and gives the reader exactly what they need to operate the TUI.
The edit-save-see loop section captures the core dev mode value proposition with a concrete, realistic scenario.
FAQ mixes all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS behavior, and comparison/choosing questions.
Skip-ahead link at the top and next-step link at the bottom satisfy the getting-started linear-flow requirements.
Lambda-to-local tunneling is called out explicitly — this is non-obvious and readers would otherwise be confused about how a Lambda function reaches a container on their laptop.
problems: Invents a specific AWS price (~$12/month for db.t4g.micro) that is not present in any of the provided source files — violates the pricing rules ('Never invent or estimate AWS list prices that are not present in the provided source').

The Agent mode section is 400+ words of HTTP endpoint documentation — appropriate for a dedicated reference page but deeply out of place in a getting-started guide aimed at first-time users who just want to see the edit-save cycle work. It buries the page's 'wow moment' and contradicts the stated audience persona.

No screenshots included. The section instructions for getting-started pages explicitly expect screenshots alongside code blocks, and the TUI-heavy nature of dev mode is particularly hard to convey without one.
Page is substantially longer than the 400–800 word getting-started target. The depth belongs in a reference or guide page; the getting-started page should surface the happy path and link out.
The `$Stage()` directive appears in the code example (`value: '$Stage()'`) with no inline explanation — a beginner has no context for what this means or whether it's required.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or replace the invented price claim. The sentence 'costs roughly ~$12/month' for db.t4g.micro has no grounding in the provided source files. Either remove the cost claim entirely or replace with a pointer: 'See current AWS RDS pricing for instance costs.' Do not leave an estimated dollar figure that contradicts the pricing rules.

Dramatically condense the Agent mode section for this getting-started page. The full endpoint table, port documentation, and daemon lifecycle explanation belong in a dedicated reference page (the page already links to '/using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev'). On this page, reduce it to 2-3 sentences: what agent mode is, how to invoke it, and a link to the full reference. This recovers roughly 400 words and keeps the page in its stated scope.
optionalImprovements: Add at least one screenshot of the dev-mode TUI in action. Even a static terminal screenshot showing the resource picker or the running-state output dramatically reduces first-time anxiety for a low-AWS-experience reader.

Add a one-clause inline explanation of `$Stage()` when it appears in the code example (e.g., a comment like 'injects the current stage name at deploy time') so a beginner is not left wondering whether it is a typo.

Clarify the 'AWS account connected' prerequisite bullet with a concrete call to action or a link to the credentials/configure step — 'AWS credentials configured (see step 1)' is much more actionable than 'connected'.

Consider trimming the FAQ to 5-6 tightly scoped entries for a getting-started context. The current 10 Q&A pairs are well-written, but the volume shifts the page closer to a reference document than an onboarding step.

The page currently introduces `--remoteResources` in two different sections (What runs where and Local database emulation) without making it clear the two mentions are the same flag. Consolidate the flag's first explanation into one place and cross-reference.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The 'What runs where' table is highly accurate against the source — container workloads, functions, SSR frameworks, hosting buckets, and databases all map correctly to what the source's getDevCompatibleResources() and getLocalEmulateableResources() enumerate.
Terminal commands table (rs, rs name, c/clear, q/quit) is exactly grounded in the handleCommand function in the source — the kind of runtime details readers need and cannot infer from config alone.

Agent mode is accurately documented: default port 7331, daemon/parent-child spawn pattern, --agent flag, and endpoint surface (status, env, rebuild, stop, AWS SDK) all match agent-server.ts precisely.
Hosting-bucket dev inclusion condition '(with dev config)' correctly reflects the hostingBuckets.filter((b) => b.dev) guard in the source — a subtle detail many pages would silently omit.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying-AWS, choosing/comparison) with concrete, non-padded answers.
Skip-ahead Info callout and next-step link are both present, satisfying the section-level getting-started requirements.

CodeBlock uses intellisense tabs with label: 'TypeScript' — no YAML config blocks, no plain-object StacktapeConfig style, no fenced typescript blocks for config. All three forbidden patterns are avoided.
Interactive picker fallback (all selected by default) is accurately described, matching the source's defaultValues: allResources.map((r) => r.name) behavior.
problems: PRICING VIOLATION — Unsourced price estimate and instance specs in main body: 'db.t4g.micro is the smallest general-purpose instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) and costs roughly ~$12/month. For workloads that need more headroom, use db.t4g.small (2 GB RAM) or db.t4g.medium (4 GB RAM)' — none of these dollar figures or RAM specs appear in any provided source file. Pricing rules state: 'Never invent or estimate AWS list prices that are not present in the provided source.'

Page substantially exceeds the section word target: the getting-started section instruction specifies 'target 400-800 words plus code blocks and screenshots.' This draft is approximately 2,000+ words of prose, 3-4x the target. For a getting-started page meant as a hands-on wow moment, the length dilutes the impact.

Agent mode section depth is misplaced for a getting-started page: ~200 words of HTTP API detail, endpoint inventory, and stop commands belong on the dedicated /using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev page already linked. A getting-started page should point readers there rather than reproduce the content.

--freshDb, --watch, and related flags are present in the curated docs (secondary source) but not directly visible in the primary src/commands/dev/index.ts. If a source-grounding verifier checks these against the primary source, they may be flagged.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the unsourced price estimate and instance memory specs from the prose after the code example. The sentence 'db.t4g.micro is the smallest general-purpose instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) and costs roughly ~$12/month. For workloads that need more headroom, use db.t4g.small (2 GB RAM) or db.t4g.medium (4 GB RAM)' must be rewritten without dollar amounts or RAM figures that do not appear in any provided source file. Acceptable replacement: 'The instanceSize property controls the RDS compute tier; db.t4g.micro is the entry-level option. In dev mode the instance size is irrelevant — the database runs as a local Docker container regardless. See the relational-database docs for the full list of supported instance sizes.' This respects the source boundary and avoids inventing prices.
optionalImprovements: Trim the agent mode section to 2-3 sentences plus the launch command, and drop the endpoint list summary. The link to /using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev does the work. This alone removes ~150 words from a page already 3x its target length.

Consider folding the 'Useful flags' and 'Terminal commands' tables into the relevant sections ('Start dev mode' and 'The edit-save-see loop') rather than as standalone late-page sections. Readers who've worked through the page will have lost context by the time they reach a standalone flags table.

Add a single screenshot of the dev mode TUI (mentioned in the section instruction as part of '400-800 words plus code blocks and screenshots'). Even a static image of the resource-picker or the running TUI would ground the page experientially.

The 'Legacy mode' section and the FAQ entry 'What's the difference between normal and legacy dev mode?' contain nearly identical content. Either compress the H2 to a pointer, or remove the FAQ entry — the duplication adds length without adding information.

In the FAQ, the answer to 'What happens if I change your Stacktape config?' could mislead users into thinking all config changes require a stack delete. Narrow the answer: only infra/schema changes (new resources, new permissions) require a delete; code-only changes (entry file path tweaks, handler logic) do not.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong answer-first paragraph (57 words, self-contained) — directly extractable by AI overviews without needing surrounding context.

'What runs where' table is excellent: every resource type is named, the local vs AWS split is concrete, and the rebuild mechanism is spelled out per row. Tables like this get cited 2–4x more than prose equivalents.
Shell blocks follow one-command-per-block discipline throughout — no copy-paste confusion, no # comments leaking into the buffer.
Agent mode details are precisely sourced: port 7331 and all endpoint categories (status, logs, env, rebuild, stop, health, DB queries, AWS SDK) match the agent-server.ts source exactly.
Terminal commands table (rs, rs <name>, c/clear, q/quit) is grounded verbatim in the handleCommand source function.
The edit-save-see loop section gives the reader a concrete mental model of the feedback cycle, explaining both the --watch and manual rs paths.
instanceSize property in the CodeBlock is explained with alternatives (db.t4g.small, db.t4g.medium) — readers understand the knob rather than copy-pasting a magic value.
Skip-ahead callout and next-step footer follow the getting-started section rhythm: users entering mid-flow or returning are served equally.
problems: FAQ fails the three-category requirement: all 10 questions are either Stacktape-specific or one comparison. There are zero use-case questions about the underlying AWS services (e.g., 'How much do Lambda invocations cost during development?', 'What Docker version or runtime is required?', 'How fast does a Lambda cold start in dev mode?'). This is a required category, and its absence means AI search engines miss the high-intent AWS queries that would bring readers to this page.

The prose explicitly invents unsourced pricing: 'db.t4g.micro is the smallest general-purpose instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) and costs roughly ~$12/month.' Neither the dollar figure nor the vCPU/RAM values appear in the provided source files (dev/index.ts, agent-server.ts, curated docs). This violates the pricing rules directly.

Page length substantially exceeds the 400-800 word target set in the getting-started section instructions. The agent mode section, the detailed debugging section, and the 10-question FAQ together push the page well past 1,500 words of prose, making this feel more like a reference page than a hands-on getting-started step.

No screenshots are included, despite the getting-started section instructions explicitly calling for 'code blocks and screenshots.' A screenshot of the dev-mode TUI showing the resource picker or the running workload panel would materially help the reader understand what to expect.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the invented pricing claim. The sentence 'db.t4g.micro is the smallest general-purpose instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) and costs roughly ~$12/month' contains price and spec data not present in any provided source file. Replace with a note like: 'In dev mode the instance size doesn't matter — the database runs as a local Docker container regardless. Choose the right size when you deploy.' and omit the dollar amount entirely.

Add at least 2 FAQ entries covering AWS-service use-case questions to satisfy the three-category mix requirement. Suggested additions: 'How much do Lambda invocations cost during development?' (answer: Lambda billed at standard AWS rates per invocation — negligible for development traffic, typically sub-cent per day) and 'What Docker version or runtime does dev mode require?' (answer: Docker Desktop or any OCI-compatible runtime such as Podman or Colima; version constraints not documented by Stacktape beyond Docker being required for containers and local database emulation).
optionalImprovements: Add at least one screenshot of the dev mode TUI — either the interactive resource picker or the running workload panel — to match the getting-started section's 'code blocks and screenshots' guidance and give first-time users a concrete visual anchor.

Trim the Agent mode section to 2-3 sentences and a single command, then link to the dedicated /using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev page for the endpoint reference. This brings the page length closer to the 400-800 word getting-started target and keeps the 'wow moment' focus rather than turning into a reference page.
In the Useful flags table, add an inline note to the --devMode row (e.g., 'See Legacy mode section') so readers who land directly on the flags table can navigate without scrolling.

The 'Debugging' section is two bullet points that add little over the linked pages. For a getting-started page, reduce it to a single sentence pointing to the debug:logs CLI page and the dev mode overview, or omit it entirely — the reader's next task is deploying, not debugging.

issues.statement: Container workloads stop, rebuild, and restart. Lambda functions repackage and update on AWS. Frontends handle their own watching natively regardless of this flag.
issues.reason: The source provides `rebuildWorkload` and `rebuildAllWorkloads` functions but does not describe the exact stop/rebuild/restart sequence for containers, nor confirm that `--watch` is irrelevant to frontends. The curated docs say '--watch' means 'auto-rebuild' without specifying per-resource-type behavior. These are reasonable inferences from the architecture but not explicitly grounded.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften the claims slightly: 'Container workloads rebuild automatically' (without specifying stop/restart sequence). For frontends, say 'SSR frontends use their framework dev server, which handles file watching independently' without claiming --watch has no effect.

issues.statement: If you change your config in a way that affects the dev stack (new resources, new permissions), delete the existing dev stack with `stacktape delete --stage dev --region eu-west-1` and restart dev mode to create a fresh one.
issues.reason: The source shows dev stack deployment is skipped when `stackManager.existingStackDetails` already exists, but does not explicitly state that config changes require deletion and recreation. It's a logical inference (since there's no update path shown), but the source is silent on this operational guidance. The advice might be correct but is not directly supported.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften to: 'If the change requires new AWS infrastructure (new IAM permissions, new secrets), you may need to restart dev mode. If the existing dev stack is insufficient, delete it and restart.' Avoid presenting deletion as the definitive required action.

issues.statement: Can I run dev mode without Docker? ... If your config has only Lambda functions with no local databases, dev mode works without Docker.
issues.reason: The source shows Docker is used for containers and database emulators, and Lambda functions run on AWS. The inference that Docker is unnecessary for Lambda-only configs is architecturally logical but not explicitly stated in the source. The source doesn't include a Docker-requirement check conditional on resource types.
issues.suggestedFix: Qualify with: 'Docker is primarily needed for container workloads and local database emulation. If your config has only Lambda functions, Docker is likely not required, though your mileage may vary.'

issues.statement: SSR frontends (Next.js, Astro, Nuxt, SvelteKit, SolidStart, TanStack Start, Remix) | Local dev server | Automatic hot reload via the framework's built-in HMR
issues.reason: The source categorizes these as 'nextjs-web' and 'ssr-web' dev-compatible resources but does not explicitly mention HMR or hot module replacement. The curated docs only say they are 'Served from a local dev server'. HMR is a reasonable assumption for these frameworks' dev servers, but it's framework behavior, not a Stacktape guarantee.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to: 'Runs the framework's local dev server (changes are handled by the framework's native dev tooling)' — this is accurate without making a specific HMR claim that the Stacktape source doesn't guarantee.

positiveFindings: All cross-links verified against the navigation index — no invented routes.
The `--agent` flag behavior, default port 7331, and daemon spawning are all accurately described and match the source.
Terminal commands (rs, rs <name>, c/clear, q/quit) exactly match the handleCommand function in the source.
The interactive resource picker, --resources all, and comma-separated resource names are all correctly described per the source.
Legacy mode behavior (requiring existing stack, no local emulation) accurately reflects the source's conditional logic.
The --noTunnel, --remoteResources, --freshDb flags are all confirmed in the source.
Environment variable naming convention (STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING) correctly follows the documented pattern with the example resource name.
The dev stack lifecycle (deploy on first run, reuse on subsequent runs) is accurately described per the source's existingStackDetails check.
Agent mode endpoint list (status, logs, env, rebuild, stop, health, database queries, AWS SDK) comprehensively matches the addRoute calls in agent-server.ts.
The 'What you need' prerequisites and overall page flow follow the getting-started linear progression correctly.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. The core dev mode behavior — resource selection, dev stack deployment, agent mode, terminal commands, local database emulation, legacy mode, and CLI flags — all accurately reflect the source code. The only issues are low-severity ambiguous claims about framework HMR behavior, the exact container rebuild sequence, Docker requirements for Lambda-only configs, and operational guidance about deleting dev stacks on config changes. None of these contradict the source; they are reasonable inferences that the source doesn't explicitly confirm. No high-severity or medium-severity factual errors were found.

The draft is broadly aligned with the provided dev-mode sources on resource selection, normal versus legacy mode, local database emulation, terminal commands, and agent-mode endpoints. The main factual risks are overbroad claims about Lambda functions running locally, missing caveats for remote or legacy database behavior, unsupported frontend hot-reload guarantees, unsupported Docker-runtime alternatives, and a few pricing/deploy claims that are not grounded in the provided sources.

issues.statement: The opening paragraph says, “Stacktape dev mode runs your workloads locally,” while also presenting Lambda functions as part of the same one-command local setup.
issues.reason: The source distinguishes local workloads from Lambda functions: containers and frontends are started locally, while functions are deployed/streamed from AWS. Because the opening paragraph is the page’s self-contained answer paragraph, this overstates what runs locally.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite the opening to say that dev mode runs local-compatible workloads locally, emulates supported databases locally, and deploys/updates Lambda functions in AWS while wiring them together for local iteration.

evidence.quote: * 5. Start local workloads (containers, frontends)
* 6. Deploy and stream logs for functions
- **Lambda functions** - Deployed to AWS, but with logs streamed to your terminal

issues.statement: “In dev mode, the instance size doesn't matter — the database runs as a local Docker container regardless.”
issues.reason: This is too absolute. In legacy mode, local database emulation is not offered, and in normal mode a database named in remote resources is not registered as a local resource.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to default normal dev mode: “In normal dev mode, selected databases run locally unless you mark them as remote with `--remoteResources`; legacy mode uses deployed resources instead.”

issues.statement: The draft claims SSR frontends and hosting buckets get “automatic hot reload” or “built-in HMR” with “no rebuild step needed.”
issues.reason: The provided source establishes that Next.js and SSR web resources are dev-compatible and that hosting buckets with dev config are selectable, but it does not document automatic HMR behavior for every listed framework or hosting bucket.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that supported frontend resources are served through local dev servers, and avoid promising HMR for every framework unless a source file for the frontend runners documents that behavior.

- **Static sites** (hosting-bucket, Next.js) - Served from a local dev server

issues.statement: “Docker Desktop or a compatible runtime (Podman, Colima) must be running.”
issues.reason: The provided sources mention Docker containers and Docker-backed database emulation, but they do not document Podman or Colima compatibility. This is a concrete compatibility claim that could affect setup decisions.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the requirement to Docker, or phrase cautiously: “Use Docker or another runtime only if it is compatible with the Docker behavior your project needs.”

evidence.quote: - **Containers** (web-service, worker-service) - Run as Docker containers on your machine
Dev mode automatically spins up Docker containers for your databases:

issues.statement: The draft gives exact RDS sizing and pricing guidance: “db.t4g.micro is the smallest general-purpose instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) and costs roughly ~$12/month,” plus RAM values for `db.t4g.small` and `db.t4g.medium`.
issues.reason: No provided source file contains RDS instance sizing or pricing data. The claim may be generally plausible AWS context, but the prompt’s pricing rules require concrete pricing to come from provided sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the dollar amount and detailed instance-size comparison, or replace it with a cautious note that production database size affects AWS RDS cost while local dev mode uses local emulation by default.

issues.statement: “A full stacktape deploy creates the complete production-grade infrastructure (load balancers, auto-scaling, CDN, alarms).”
issues.reason: The provided dev-mode sources do not describe `stacktape deploy` behavior, and the listed infrastructure depends on the user’s `stacktape.ts` resources. As written, the sentence implies these components are created broadly by every deploy.
issues.suggestedFix: Change it to: “A full `stacktape deploy` creates the AWS resources described by your `stacktape.ts`; depending on your config, that can include load balancers, CDN, scaling settings, and alarms.”

positiveFindings: The page uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab for Stacktape configuration and does not hand-author YAML.
The `--resources`, `--skipResources`, interactive picker, `rs`, `rs name`, `c`/`clear`, and `q`/`quit` descriptions match the provided dev command source.
The normal versus legacy mode explanation is mostly grounded: normal mode deploys a dev stack when needed, while legacy mode requires an existing stack.
The agent-mode port and endpoint summary are well supported by `agent-server.ts` and `dev/index.ts`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main dev-mode surface well for a Getting Started page: resource selection, normal vs legacy mode, local database emulation, terminal rebuild commands, agent mode, and the next-step flow. I found two completeness/precision issues worth fixing before publication.

issues.statement: The Agent mode section explains `--agent` but does not document that the port can be chosen with `--agentPort` / `-ap`.
issues.reason: The source shows agent mode defaults to port 7331 or the next available port, and also supports an explicit `agentPort` argument. Readers using AI agents or multiple local dev sessions are likely to need deterministic port selection.
issues.suggestedFix: Add one sentence and one command after the default-port paragraph: “Use `--agentPort` when a tool expects a stable port.” Then show `stacktape dev --stage dev --region eu-west-1 --resources all --agent --agentPort 7331`.

evidence.quote: const agentPortArg = globalStateManager.args.agentPort; const agentEnabled = Boolean(globalStateManager.args.agent || agentPortArg !== undefined);
hint: 'Specify a port with --agentPort or free up ports in that range.'

issues.statement: The page does not mention that `--skipResources` can be used by itself to run all resources except the excluded ones.
issues.reason: The Useful flags table lists `--skipResources`, but the examples only show `--resources all` and `--resources name,name`. The source explicitly supports a `--skipResources`-only path, which is useful in monorepos and large stacks where most resources should run except one expensive or irrelevant workload.
issues.suggestedFix: In the “Start dev mode” or “Useful flags” section, add a short example: `stacktape dev --stage dev --region eu-west-1 --skipResources searchIndexer` and explain that this runs all selectable resources except the named ones.

evidence.quote: // Case 3: --skipResources only (run all except skipped)
return new Set([...allNames].filter((n) => !skipSet.has(n)));

positiveFindings: The draft preserves the Getting Started sequence and ends with the required next-step link.
The documented terminal commands match the command handler: `rs`, `rs <name>`, `c` / `clear`, and `q` / `quit`.
The resource-selection behavior is covered accurately: interactive picker by default, `--resources all`, and comma-separated resource names.
Agent mode coverage names the key endpoint families present in `agent-server.ts`, including status, logs, env vars, rebuild, stop, local-resource health, database helpers, and AWS SDK access.
The Stacktape configuration example uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with `defineConfig` and a `TypeScript` tab, avoiding the forbidden YAML and `StacktapeConfig` patterns.

verifierResults.summary: Deterministic check of <CodeBlock intellisense> Stacktape config examples and generated MDX component usage.

issues.statement: <CodeBlock intellisense> #1 (TypeScript): cannot generate YAML tab from TypeScript source.
issues.reason: The TypeScript example parsed and type-checked, but the docs YAML converter could not derive a YAML tab. This may be a docs-converter coverage gap for valid class-based config.
issues.suggestedFix: If the example is valid Stacktape TypeScript, extend the YAML converter for this class/property shape. Otherwise simplify the example to a converter-supported class-based defineConfig shape.

issues.statement: <CodeBlock intellisense> #1 (TypeScript): `new RdsEnginePostgresql16(...)` references an unknown class.
issues.reason: `RdsEnginePostgresql16` is not exported by the `stacktape` package.
issues.suggestedFix: Use one of the documented Stacktape resource classes (e.g. LambdaFunction, WebService, RelationalDatabase). If `RdsEnginePostgresql16` is meant to be a custom class, define it in the example or remove the example.
